coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots derived from lysine and arginine simultaneously scavenge bacteria and promote tissue repair
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Peili
Han, Fengxuan
Cao, Weiwei
Zhang, Gaoke
Li, Jiaying
Zhou, Jinwei
Gong, Xuedong
Turnbull, Gareth
Shu, Wenmiao
Xia, Lunguo
Fang, Bing
Xing, Xiaodong
Li, Bin
coredata.dc:description: Effectively targeting and killing microbial pathogens is a major challenge in the treatment of infectious diseases. Inspired by the high efficacy of natural antimicrobial peptides (AMPs), in this study we prepared two types of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) by pyrolysis of lysine and arginine, the two major functional moieties of natural AMPs. Both lysine-derived CQDs (Lys-CQDs) and arginine-derived CQDs (Arg-CQDs) exhibited distinctive antibacterial activity against both Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria without inducing drug resistance. They also effectively inhibited the formation of bacterial biofilms. Such excellent antibacterial properties might be the result of positive charges derived from the amino acid residues on CQD surface and the generation of rich intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS). Surprisingly, instead of being cytotoxic, CQDs dramatically promoted the growth of typical mammalian cells. Moreover, no hemolysis of red blood cells (RBCs) happened when CQD concentration was less than 4,000 μg/mL. In vivo, administration of CQDs to infected wounds effectively inhibited bacterium growth and accelerated wound healing. Together, findings from this study indicate that Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs can simultaneously kill bacteria and facilitate cell growth, and may therefore be promising candidates for treating bacterial infections and promoting tissue repair.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Antibacterial
Non-hemolytic
Biocompatible
Wound healing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of carbon quantum dots from lysine and arginine
Detecting reactive oxygen species induced by CQDs
Detecting the total SOD activities of E. coli, NIH 3T3 and red blood cells (RBCs)
Detecting the catalase (CAT) activities of E. coli, NIH 3T3 and red blood cells
Inhibiting S. aureus biofilm formation assays
Live/dead staining of cells treated with CQDs
Wound healing on the backs of mice
Preparation and characterizations of Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs
Antibacterial activities and antibacterial mechanism of CQDs
In vitro hemolytic activity, cytotoxicity and cell selectivity
Wound healing application and in vivo antibacterial ability

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China-Yunnan Joint Fund

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Molecular basis of the cell selectivity of CQDs.

(A) HRTEM images and particle size distributions of Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of lysine, arginine and Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs, respectively. The insets show Lys-CQD (above) and Arg-CQD (below) solutions under ambient light (left) and 365 nm UV radiation (right), respectively. Fluorescence (FL) spectra of (C) Lys-CQDs and (D) Arg-CQDs, respectively.

(A) SEM images of E. coli and S. aureus before treatment (Control) and after treatment with Lys-CQDs (500 μg/mL) and Arg-CQDs (500 μg/mL) for 40 min. The concentrations of CQDs were 500 μg/mL. Scale bars, 1 μm. Time-kill curves of (B) Lys-CQDs and (C) Arg-CQDs for S. aureus and E. coli, respectively. Killing activity was monitored for the first 4 h upon contact with CQDs.

(A) ROS generation in E. coli, NIH 3T3 mouse fibroblast cells and RBCs, respectively, when incubated with Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs. The amount of ROS was proportional to the fluorescent intensity of DCF, which was excited at a wavelength of 488 nm. CQD concentrations were 500 μg/mL. (B) Inhibition rate of total SOD activity of E. coli, NIH 3T3 and RBCs, respectively, by Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs at different concentrations. (C) Inhibition rate of CAT activity of E. coli, NIH 3T3 and RBCs, respectively, by Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs at different concentrations.

(A) Drug-resistance study of Lys-CQDs, Arg-CQDs, and Rifampin against E. Coli and S. aureus. Biofilm was formed by S. aureus. (B) SEM and CLSM images showing inhibition of biofilm formation by Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs. (C) Biofilm inhibition rates for Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs by crystal violet assay.

Hemolytic activities of (A) Lys-CQD and (B) Arg-CQD (0.49–4000 μg/mL) solutions on RBCs in PBS (pH = 7.4). The OD576 values of the mixtures, where the RBCs are treated with the as-prepared CQDs for (a) 1 h and (b) 24 h with agitation at 37 °C, are detected by a UV–vis spectrophotometer. (c) Hemolysis percentages of CQDs with different concentrations and (d) photographs of the corresponding RBC solutions (treated with CQDs for 1 h). Hemolysis assays with PBS and ultrapure water as a negative control and positive control, respectively.

(A) Cytotoxicity tests of different concentrations of Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs on NIH 3T3 cells after incubation for 1, 3, 5 and 7 d, respectively. The initial cell density was 1 × 104 cells per well. The OD450 values measured using a microplate reader indirectly quantified the number of viable cells. The error bars represent the standard deviation of six duplicate measurements. (B) Merged fluorescence images for live/dead staining of NIH 3T3 mouse fibroblast cells treated with different concentrations of Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs for 24 h. The cell density was 1 × 105 cells per well. Scale bars, 400 μm.

(A) Photographs of wounds on the backs of mice from nine groups at different times (scale bars,10 mm). (B) Histologic analysis of wound tissue using H&E staining. The tissues (B1–B10) indicated by a red box are magnified under the corresponding picture. (C) Quantification of S. aureus bacteria and E. coli surviving in wound tissue was performed at 1 and 5 days. (D) Detection of hydrogen peroxide in the wound on the backs of the mice after 5 days of bacterial infection. Control (wound), S. aureus (infected with S. aureus), E. coli (infected with E. coli), S. aureus + band-aid (treated with 3 M bandage after planting S. aureus), E. coli + band-aid (treated with 3 M bandage after planting E. coli), S. aureus + Lys-CQDs (treated with Lys-CQDs after infection with S. aureus), E. coli + Lys-CQDs (treated with Lys-CQDs after infection with E. coli), S. aureus + Arg-CQDs (treated with Arg-CQDs after infection with S. aureus), and E. coli + Arg-CQDs (treated with Arg-CQDs after infection with E. coli). The concentrations of S. aureus and E. coli were 8.3 × 108 and 2.3 × 108 CFU/mL, respectively. The concentration of CQDs was 2 mg/mL. (**p < 0.01 compared with blank group).
Minimum inhibitory concentrations and selectivity index values of CQDs.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The selectivity index is obtained by dividing the HC10 by the MIC value for a given bacterium at pH 7.4.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots derived from lysine and arginine simultaneously scavenge bacteria and promote tissue repair

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing 210094, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanjing University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, The First Affiliated Hospital of Soochow University, Suzhou, Jiangsu 215006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The First Affiliated Hospital of Soochow University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Orthopaedic Institute, Soochow University, Suzhou, Jiangsu 215006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biomedical Engineering, University of Strathclyde, Glasgow, G1 1QE, United Kingdom

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Orthodontics, Ninth People's Hospital Affiliated to Shanghai Jiao Tong University, School of Medicine, Shanghai, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ninth People’s Hospital Affiliated to Shanghai Jiao Tong University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: f
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: China Orthopaedic Regenerative Medicine Group (CORMed), Hangzhou, Zhejiang 310000, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Effectively targeting and killing microbial pathogens is a major challenge in the treatment of infectious diseases. Inspired by the high efficacy of natural antimicrobial peptides (AMPs), in this study we prepared two types of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) by pyrolysis of lysine and arginine, the two major functional moieties of natural AMPs. Both lysine-derived CQDs (Lys-CQDs) and arginine-derived CQDs (Arg-CQDs) exhibited distinctive antibacterial activity against both Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria without inducing drug resistance. They also effectively inhibited the formation of bacterial biofilms. Such excellent antibacterial properties might be the result of positive charges derived from the amino acid residues on CQD surface and the generation of rich intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS). Surprisingly, instead of being cytotoxic, CQDs dramatically promoted the growth of typical mammalian cells. Moreover, no hemolysis of red blood cells (RBCs) happened when CQD concentration was less than 4,000 μg/mL. In vivo, administration of CQDs to infected wounds effectively inhibited bacterium growth and accelerated wound healing. Together, findings from this study indicate that Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs can simultaneously kill bacteria and facilitate cell growth, and may therefore be promising candidates for treating bacterial infections and promoting tissue repair.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infection is one of the major global challenges to public health [1]. Due to excessive use of antibiotics, resistant bacteria rapidly emerge [2,3]. One of the most promising strategies for overcoming the drug resistance of bacteria is the use of antibacterial nanomaterials [4]. For any antibacterial agent, a major concern in clinical applications is its cytotoxicity [5]. While numerous efforts have been made for balancing antimicrobial activity with biological safety [6,7], reducing off-target adverse effects during antimicrobial administration remains a significant challenge [8]. Therefore, the development of nanomaterials with preferential activity against bacteria over mammalian cells is needed.

Recent advances in carbon-based nanomaterials have led to new hope for combating infection. Unlike commonly used metal and metal oxide nanoparticles, carbon-based nanomaterials, including fullerene [9], nanodiamonds [10], carbon nanotubes [11], and graphene and its derivatives [12,13], have relatively low side effects to mammalian cells and tissues [14,15]. Among them, recently emerged zero-dimensional carbon quantum dots (CQDs) possess excellent fluorescence, very small size, environmental friendly and low-cost synthesis, decent water dispersibility, low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility [16]. These aspects make CQDs particularly attractive in numerous applications, including targeted drug delivery for antitumor treatment [17], biosensors [18], photocatalysis [19], light-emitting diodes [20], bioimaging [21] and fluorescent labeling [22]. Recently, CQDs with bactericidal activity have been reported, likely due to their potential to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) [23,24]. Therefore, CQDs have been considered promising antibacterial agents for treating bacteria-associated infections.

CQDs, depending on the surface charges, show distinct responses to bacteria and cells [24]. The endogenous ROS induced by positively charged CQDs is obviously higher than that of negatively charged dots. Moreover, positively charged dots can disrupt bacterial membrane. As a result, positively charged spermine CQDs show bactericidal activity, and candle-soot CQDs exhibit bacteriostatic ability, yet uncharged glucose CQDs hardly induce the generation of ROS, and are inactive on bacteria cells. The bactericidal activity of CQDs is also related to their surface chemistry and size [25]. For nitrogen-doped CQDs, the electrostatic interactions mainly contribute to their bacterial toxicity, and the ability to activate ROS also plays a possible role. Sulfur-doped CQDs with negatively charged surfaces show size-dependent growth inhibition of bacteria, while spermidine and spermidine-capped CQDs with super cationic features are highly bactericidal, likely as a result of disruption of bacterial membrane by the highly positive charge [26,27]. In contrast to other findings, no ROS production has been observed in this system because polyamines can protect against oxidative damage through ROS scavenging mechanism. However, the exact mechanisms of ROS, surface charges and surface chemistry on the bactericidal activity of CQDs remain to be clarified. On the other hand, while CQDs generally exhibit good biocompatibility and may even selectively target bacteria with negligible cytotoxicity toward host cells within a certain concentration range [28,29], when the concentration of CQDs increases, normal mammalian cells are inevitably damaged. Hemolysis of red blood cells (RBCs) has also observed, which limits the potential use of CQDs in antibacterial treatments [27].

ROS plays a critical role in biological functions. As signal molecules, ROS regulates many signal transduction pathways by reacting with and modifying proteins, transcription factors and gene structures to regulate various cellular functions [30]. Moderate increase in the amount of ROS can promote cell proliferation and differentiation, while excessive ROS can cause cell senescence and even cell death through oxidative damage to proteins, lipids and DNA [30,31]. ROS induced by CQDs effectively inhibits bacterial growth [25]. On the other hand, positively charged CQDs also possess good antibacterial capabilities due to their cationic disruption of cell membrane structures [26].

Inspired by the antibacterial function of the lysine and arginine amino acid residues discovered in natural antimicrobial peptides (AMPs) [32], in this study we used l-lysine and l-arginine as raw materials to synthesize positively charged CQDs through a facile pyrolysis process (Scheme S1). Such cationic residues-incorporated CQDs might exhibit similar antibacterial effects as AMPs and could effectively disrupt bacterial membranes with minimal side effects on cells [33]. As expected, in the in vitro studies both Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs showed significant antibacterial properties. More importantly, these CQDs showed the ability to promote the growth of typical mammalian cells. As a result, they better faciliated the healing of wounds in mice subjected to antibacterial administration. To the best of our knowledge, although CQDs are widely known to be biocompatible, this is the first report that an antibacterial agent can indeed promote cell growth.

L-Lysine (Lys), l-Arginine (Arg), sodium chloride, monopotassium phosphate, disodium hydrogen phosphate, potassium chloride, ortho-nitrophenyl-β-d-galactopyranoside (ONPG), and rifampicin were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., LTD (Nanjing, China). Two strains of Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, ATCC 25923 and Escherichia coli (E. coli, ATCC 25922 were collected from Nanjing Lezhen Biotechnology Co., LTD (Nanjing, China). The LB media and tryptic soy broth (TSB) media were obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). All culture media for NIH 3T3, for example, penicillin-streptomycin, were purchased from Gibco BRL (Grand Island, NY, USA). The SYTO Green-Fluorescent Nucleic Acid Stains assay kit was purchased from Molecular Probes (Eugene, OR, USA). The B-PER bacterial protein extraction reagent (bacteria lysis agent) was purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. (Waltham, MA, USA). The cytotoxicity was quantified using a live/dead viability/cytotoxicity kit (Invitrogen, CA, USA). The Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) was purchased from Dojindo, Kumamoto. Ultrapure water (18 MΩ) was used in all the experiments. Phosphate buffered saline (PBS; containing 10 mM Na2HPO4, 2.0 mM KH2PO4, 137 mM NaCl, and 5.0 mM KCl; pH 7.4) served to mimic physiological conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon quantum dots from lysine and arginine
ce:section.ce:para: Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs were synthesized using a previously reported pyrolytic procedure [26]. A simple heating method was used to prepare CQDs from lysine and arginine. Briefly, 500 mg of lysine or arginine powder was placed on a corundum crucible and heated in a pipe furnace at 240 °C for 3 h. After the furnace naturally cooled to ambient temperature, the residue was dissolved in 20 mL ultrapure water. The CQD solution was sonicated for 30 min and then centrifuged at 15000 rpm for 30 min at room temperature. Then, the supernatant was dialyzed in ultrapure water with a dialysis membrane MWCO = 1000 Da for 24 h; the ultrapure water was replaced each hour. Finally, CQD powder was obtained by vacuum freeze-drying for 48 h and kept in cold storage at 4 °C.

The high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were obtained at 200 kV using a transmission electron microscope (JEM-2100, JEOL Ltd, Japan). XRD (Bruker D8 ADVANCE, Germany) was used to analyze the phase purity and crystallinity of CQDs with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm), a target voltage of 40 kV, and a current of 40 mA. The ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs were detected from 200 to 800 nm using a Biomate 3S spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), and fluorescence spectroscopy was performed with an F-2700 fluorescence (FL) spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The emission spectra were recorded in the wavelength range of 300–700 nm. The Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) of samples were recorded from 525 to 4000 cm−1 by a Nicolet iS10 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The surface elemental compositions of CQDs were identified by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) equipped with an axial ultra-DLD device (Krados, UK) with Al Kα excitation. The zeta potentials (ζ) of Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs were assessed using a Zetasizer (Nano ZS, Malvern Instruments, Worcestershire, UK).

Gram-negative E. coli and Gram-positive S. aureus were used as model pathogens for antibacterial activity tests. In LB liquid media, bacteria were proliferated overnight at 37 °C. Subsequently, the bacteria were collected by centrifugation (RCF 3500 g, 10 min, 20 °C), washed twice with PBS, and diluted to 105–106 CFU/mL. CQDs were added to the LB medium in test tubes to obtain a concentration of 2 mg/mL. From the initial solution, a series of CQDs solutions were obtained by two-fold dilution. Subsequently, 10 μL of 105–106 CFU/mL bacteria suspension was added to each test tube. The control was bacteria suspension in an LB medium without CQDs, and the blank was only LB medium. The mixtures were incubated at 37 °C with agitation at 200 rpm. Each test tube was visually inspected for turbidity after incubation for 24 h. The concentration of the CQDs in the last clear test tube was the MIC value. Each value was repeated three times to for averaging [34].

The antibacterial kinetic data of Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs against E. coli and S. aureus were measured [34]. The E. coli and S. aureus bacteria were incubated to mid-logarithmic phase in LB media to obtain suspensions of 106 and 107 CFU/mL. Each bacterial suspension was cultured with different concentrations of Lys-CQDs or Arg-CQDs (125 μg/mL, 250 μg/mL, and 500 μg/mL) for 0 min, 10 min, 30 min, 1 h, 2 h, 3 h and 4 h. At each time point, the mixture was diluted with sterilized PBS (pH = 7.4); then, 10-fold serial dilutions were carried out, and the mixtures were spread on LB agar plates. The colonies on each plate were counted and plotted against the culture time after cultivation at 37 °C for 18–20 h.

The 1 × 108 CFU/mL bacterial suspensions of E. coli and S. aureus (1000 μL) were centrifuged at RCF 3500g for 10 min to remove the liquid media. The bacteria cells were washed with PBS (pH 7.4) three times. Subsequently, 100 μL of the E. coli or S. aureus suspension (107 CFU/mL) was cultured with Lys-CQDs or Arg-CQDs (500 μg/mL) in PBS (pH 7.4) for 40 min. Next, the bacteria were fixed to a cover slip in 6-well palates with 2.5% glutaraldehyde for 24 h. Then, the samples were dehydrated with 30%, 50%, 70%, 80%, 90%, 95% and 100% ethanol solutions. The samples were dried with a critical point drying instrument. The morphology of the bacteria was observed by field emission scanning electron microscopy after gold spraying [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detecting reactive oxygen species induced by CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The generation of intercellular ROS was monitored by measuring the transformation of 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescin-diacetate (DCFH-DA) to fluorescent 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein (DCF) because non-fluorescent DCFH-DA can be deacetylated to produce the nonfluorescent 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein by cellular esterases and then oxidized to fluorescent DCF by ROS. DCF shows a fluorescent emission peak at 525 nm when excited at 488 nm. Briefly, 300 μL E. coli at a concentration of 1 × 108 CFU/mL (centrifuged and washed three times with sterile water) was added to each sterile vial. Next, 2 mL of DCFH-DA (10 μM) or sterile water (control) was added. The mixtures were then incubated at 37 °C for 20 min in a shaker. Subsequently, Lys-CQDs (500 μg/mL), Arg-CQDs (500 μg/mL), positive control (1 mM H2O2), or negative control (sterile water) was added to the vials. The total volume of each vial was 3 mL. The samples were cultured at 37 °C for 30 min in a shaker. Finally, the fluorescent intensity was measured at an excitation wavelength of 488 nm using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The ROS content was directly proportional to the fluorescent intensity of the DCF [35]. The detection processes of ROS in NIH 3T3 cells and red blood cells were the same as above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detecting the total SOD activities of E. coli, NIH 3T3 and red blood cells (RBCs)
Detecting the catalase (CAT) activities of E. coli, NIH 3T3 and red blood cells
ce:section.ce:para: In the case of relatively sufficient hydrogen peroxide, CAT can turn hydrogen peroxide into water and oxygen. Residual hydrogen peroxide oxidizes the chromogenic substrate under peroxidase catalysis to produce a red product (N-(4-antipyryl)-3-chloro-5-sulfonate-pbenzoquinonemonoimine) with a maximum absorption wavelength of 520 nm. A standard curve was prepared using hydrogen peroxide standards. The sample without treatment by the CQDs or other drugs served as the control group, the inhibition rate of which was 0. The detection methods for the CAT activities of the NIH 3T3 cells and red blood cells were the same as above.

The initial MIC values of Lys-CQDs, Arg-CQDs and rifampicin against E. coli and S. aureus were obtained as mentioned above. Bacteria from test tubes at concentrations of 1/2 MIC were then used to make bacterial dilutions (approximately 106 CFU/mL) for the next MIC study. These mixtures were incubated with Lys-CQDs, Arg-CQDs or the control antibiotic rifampicin. After incubation at 37 °C for 24 h, the new MIC was obtained. The test was repeated each day for 14 passages [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibiting S. aureus biofilm formation assays
ce:section.ce:para:
A colony of S. aureus on the inclined plane was added to the LB medium and grown in a table concentrator for 24 h at 37 °C. Next, a TSB medium containing 1% glycerol was prepared, and the solution was divided into two bottles with 50 mL per bottle and sterilized at 121 °C for 20 min. Subsequently, 500 μL S. aureus solution was added to 50 mL TSB culture medium (1:100) with Lys-CQDs or Arg-CQDs of which the final concentrations were 0–1000 μg/mL. Biofilms were formed on cover glass in 12-well plates after each sample was statically cultured with fresh TSB at 37 °C for 24 h. (1) Fluorescence photographs from confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) [28]: the biofilms were washed three times with PBS (pH = 7.4), stained with an AO/EB staining kit (live/dead staining kit, Sangon Biotech) for 15 min. Subsequently, samples were observed under a CLSM (TCS SP8, Leica, Germany). Fluorescence images were obtained when the stained biofilm was excited at 488 and 520 nm. (2) Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) of biofilm: the biofilms were washed three times with PBS (pH = 7.4) and fixed with 2.5% glutaraldehyde for 24 h. The samples were dehydrated with 30%, 50%, 70%, 80%, 90%, 95% and 100% ethanol solutions. Then, the samples were dried with a critical point drying instrument. The morphology of the biofilm was observed by SEM after gold spraying. (3) Crystal violet assay: the inhibiting biofilm formation assay by crystal violet staining was based on a previous report with slight modification [37]. Different concentrations of Lys-CQDs or Arg-CQDs (0–2000 μg/mL) were added to 200 μL solutions of the aforementioned samples in 96-well plates. The mixtures were incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. After removing the planktonic bacteria in the supernatant, the biofilms were washed with PBS (pH = 7.4) three times. Next, 200 μL methanol was added to each well to fix the biofilm for 15 min. Then, after the methanol was removed, the samples were dried at 60 °C. The biofilm was cultured with 50 μL 0.1% crystal violet solution for 15 min. The excess crystal violet was washed with PBS (pH = 7.4) three times. After drying at 60 °C, 200 μL of glacial acetic acid was added to each well with 30 min agitation. The absorbance at 570 nm was tested with a microplate reader (BioTek, USA). The samples were tested in triplicate, and the tests were repeated three times. The inhibitory rates were computed using the following equation.
Inhibition rate ( % ) =
O
D 570
( Control ) − O
D 570
( Sample )
O
D 570
( Control )

Assays of the hemolysis induced by Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs were carried out on the basis of a previously reported process [26]. Fresh blood samples of healthy people were drawn from blood vessels into tubes containing EDTA and were centrifuged within 30 min at RCF 3000 g for 10 min to remove the serum. The red blood cells (RBCs) were collected by following the relevant institutional guidelines. The fresh RBCs were then washed several times with sterile PBS until the liquid supernatant became transparent. The RBC stock suspension (500 μL) was added to each Lys-CQD and Arg-CQD solution (PBS, 500 μL). The concentrations of CQDs ranged from 0.98 to 4000 μg/mL in 1.5 mL vials. After culturing for 1 h or 24 h at 37 °C, each vial was centrifuged for 10 min at RCF 1000 g. Finally, hemolysis activity was determined by testing the absorbance of the hemoglobin at 576 nm in the liquid supernatant (diluted 10-fold, OD576 CQDs). The sterile PBS was used as a blank for 0% hemolysis (OD576 blank). Ultrapure water was used as a control for 100% hemolysis (OD576 ultrapure water). The hemolysis activity was calculated by the formula:
Hemolysis rate ( % ) =
O
D 576
( CQDs ) − O
D 576
( PBS )
O
D 576
( ultrapure water ) − O
D 576
( PBS )

The cytotoxicity of CQDs on three typical cell types, including mouse embryonic fibroblast cell line NIH-3T3 cells, bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells (BMSCs) and human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs), was evaluated using CCK-8 tests [34]. In brief, cells were plated in 96 wells at a density of 1 × 104 cells/well. Then CQDs were supplemented to the medium at concentrations ranging from 0 to 1000 μg/mL and cell culture continued for 24 h at 37 °C. The mixtures were washed with PBS buffer three times to remove CQDs. To each well, 10 μL of CCK-8 dyes and 100 μL of fresh Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle’s Medium (DMEM) were added, and the cells were cultured at 37 °C for 2 h in the dark. Finally, the OD450 values were measured to indirectly assess the number of viable cells by a microplate reader (Victor III, Perkin-Elmer). The untreated cells were used as a positive control, and the cell survival rate was recorded as 100%. The experiment was repeated three times to obtain average values. The OD450 values of mixtures of 3, 5, and 7 d were detected the same as the process mentioned above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Live/dead staining of cells treated with CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: First, fresh Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle's medium containing 10% serum and 1% penicillin-streptomycin was prepared. Solutions containing different concentrations of CQDs ranging from 0 to 1000 μg/mL were added to a 96-cell plate containing 200 μL cell culture medium and 1 × 104 cells per well. Three types of cells were used in this study, including NIH-3T3 cells, BMSCs and HUVECs were used in this study. After culturing for 24 h, the medium was removed and washed twice with PBS (pH 7.4) to remove the excess medium. Then, 100 μL of live/dead dye solution was added to each well at room temperature for 15 min in a dark box. Finally, an inverted fluorescence microscope was used to observe the cell morphology and take fluorescence images. The control group was the cells without CQD treatment [7].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Wound healing on the backs of mice
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the antibacterial effects of Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs, a wound model was established. Forty-five male mice (6–8 weeks, 18–22 g, 5 mice per group) with a wound of approximately sixteen square millimeters (4 cm × 4 cm) were randomized into saline, bacterial infection, 3 M antibacterial bandage (benzalkonium chloride), Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs groups. The wounds were infected with 10 μL of S. aureus or E. coli at concentrations of 8.3 × 108 and 2.3 × 108 CFU/mL, respectively. Nine groups of differently treated mice were observed and photographed. After five days, the amount of hydrogen peroxide in the wound of each mouse was tested, and the wound tissue was taken. The numbers of bacteria in the wound tissues of eight groups of mice were quantified on the first and fifth days after treatment. The wound tissues were homogenized with sterile saline. Diluted homogeneous samples were coated on LB solid media, and the growth colonies were counted for histograms [38]. To understand the extent of healing, tissue samples excised from the entire wound and adjacent normal skin were histologically examined on postoperative day 5 and fixed with 10% formaldehyde solution. Specimens containing dermal and subcutaneous tissue were histologically examined by hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining [27]. All the animal experiments were in line with the guidelines of the committee on animal care and use.

All the values were represented by mean ± SD. All the records were obtained from at least three free-running tests. According to the TTEST function in Microsoft Excel, the double tail heteroscedasticity t-test was used to evaluate the statistical significance. When the p value was less than 0.05, the difference between the groups was statistically significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterizations of Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs were prepared using a simple pyrolysis process. As shown in the high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) images of CQDs, both Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs are spherical and uniformly dispersed without apparent aggregation (Fig. 1 A). The particle size ranges from 2 nm to 7 nm. Well-resolved lattice fringe with intercrystalline spacings of 0.21 nm and 0.18 nm is observed, corresponding to the (100) and (102) facets of graphitic carbon, respectively [39]. The XRD spectra of CQDs show broad diffraction peaks at 20.5° and 22.5° for Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs, corresponding to the (002) facet of graphitic carbon (Supplementary Fig. S1). The layer spacings of Lys-CQDs (0.43 nm) and Arg-CQDs (0.39 nm) are larger than that of graphite (0.33 nm), which indicate the lattice spacing of CQDs with turbostratic disorder. As depicted in Fig. 1B, band I (250 nm–300 nm) is the intrinsic absorption of the aromatic sp2 hybrid region, attributed to the π→π*. The absorbance of band II (310 nm–350 nm) is ascribed to the surface and edge defects of CQDs (n→π*). The internal illustrations in the inset of Fig. 1B are optical photographs of Lys-CQDs (above) and Arg-CQDs (below) under 365 nm ultraviolet light illumination. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 1(C and D), the photoluminescence spectra of the synthetic CQDs are broad, trailing and dependent on the excitation wavelength. Upon excitation at 340 nm, Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs dispersions emit the strongest fluorescence centered at 411 and 397 nm, respectively. According to the inset normalized graph, an obvious redshift is observed in the emission along with the variation of the excitation from 300 to 500 nm.

The chemical features of the CQDs were identified using FT-IR spectroscopy. As depicted in Supplementary Fig. S2, peaks at 1653 cm−1, 1556 cm−1, and 1435 cm−1 are assigned to the amide I (CO), amide II (NH) and amide III (CN) stretching vibrations, respectively [40]. The broad bands centered at 3354 and 3328 cm−1 suggest the existence of OH and NH2, respectively. From the above results, we speculate that our CQDs retain certain characteristic functional groups of the respective amino acids. Furthermore, XPS spectra are recorded to analyze the compositions of both CQDs. Supplementary Fig. S3A and S3E indicates that the elemental contents of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen (atomic percentage) are 73.9%, 14.7%, 11.4% for Lys-CQDs, respectively, and 62.8%, 21.7%, 15.5% for Arg-CQDs, respectively. The XPS analysis shows that Lys-CQDs have more oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing groups than Arg-CQDs. The deconvoluted C 1s spectrum of Lys-CQDs (Supplementary Fig. S3B) indicates five types of carbon bonds, CC/CN (283.7 eV; 7.82%), CC (284.8 eV; 53.66%), CN (285.7 eV; 12.13%), CO (286.5 eV; 22.73%) and CO (287.6 eV; 3.66%) [41]. The N 1s spectrum (Supplementary Fig. S3C) reveals four types of nitrogen bonds, pyridinic (N-6) (398.6 eV; 42.61%), NH2 (399.3 eV; 26.55%), pyrrolic (N-5) (400 eV; 25.20%) and quaternary nitrogen (N–Q) (401 eV; 5.64%) [42]. The O 1s XPS spectrum (Supplementary Fig. S3D) shows two types of oxygen bonds, OC (530.8 eV; 71.61%) and COH/COO (532.2 eV; 28.39%) [26]. The high-resolution spectra of Arg-CQDs (Supplementary Fig. S3F, 3G, 3H) are approximately similar to that of Lys-CQDs. However, Lys-CQDs contain more N–Q (0.83%) than Arg-CQDs (0.42%). As shown in Supplementary Fig. S4, the ζ-potential of Lys-CQDs is mid-level positive (19.3 mV) at pH 7.4, while the surface charge of Arg-CQDs is slightly positive with a ζ-potential of 7.21 mV. This result can be ascribed to the high N–Q content on the Lys-CQD surface. In addition, Arg-CQDs contain more oxygen-containing groups than Lys-CQDs, which weakens the positive charge of Arg-CQDs. Next, we investigate the surface charges of CQDs at different pH. With decreasing pH values, the ζ-potential values of CQDs apparently increase (Lys: 30.8 mV at pH 6.5, 38.3 mV at pH 5.5; Arg: 15.7 mV at pH 6.5, 30.5 mV at pH 5.5), which is ascribed to the protonation of the amino groups on the CQD surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial activities and antibacterial mechanism of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The standard dilution method is used to determine the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) values of CQDs in different strains, including Gram-negative E. coli and Gram-positive S. aureus. As depicted in Table 1 , the MIC values of Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs are 62.5 μg/mL and 125 μg/mL for E. coli, respectively, and 31.25 μg/mL and 62.5 μg/mL for S. aureus, respectively (pH = 7.4). This result indicates that the as-prepared CQDs show high inhibitory activities for both Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria. Additionally, the MIC values decrease with pH decreasing from 7.4 to 5.5, which can be attributed to the increasing ζ-potentials of the CQD surface. We further confirm that the surface charges of CQDs play a vital role in determining their antibacterial potency [26]. At bacterial infection sites, the local environment becomes acidic [34]. Our CQDs exhibit enhanced bactericidal activity in acidic conditions, which favors their practical application in bacterial infection treatments. Additionally, the high selectivity (HC10/MIC) of our CQDs toward bacteria over red blood cells (RBCs) is apparent from Table 1.

In order to examine the integrity of cell membranes after CQDs treatment, live/dead staining tests of S. aureus were performed using SYTO 9/propidium iodide (PI). Clearly, bacteria treated with CQDs showed gradual increase in the red fluorescence of PI, indicating that the bacterial membranes were destroyed with time (Supplementary Fig. S5). After 1 h of treatment with Lys-CQDs, no living bacteria could be identified. Meanwhile, FE-SEM was used to observe the morphology changes in E. coli and S. aureus after incubation with CQDs. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the membranes of untreated bacteria were intact and smooth. After incubation with both CQDs, the structural integrity of the cell was markedly destroyed, leading to leakage of bacterial cytoplasm. To further check the integrity of bacterial membranes, ortho-nitrophenyl-β-d-galactopyranoside (ONPG) was used to detect the level of cytoplasmic β-galactosidase released from E. coli after CQD treatment. As shown in Supplementary Fig. S6, the absorbance of mixtures treated with Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs is 0.5 and 0.47, respectively, indicating high membrane damage efficiency of Lys-CQDs. Therefore, CQDs could penetrate the plasma membrane and lead to cytoplasm leakage, which is consistent with FE-SEM observations. Moreover, the dehydrogenase activity of bacteria was obviously inhibited after incubation with Lys-CQDs or Arg-CQDs for 2 h, meaning that CQDs affected the pyridine-nucleoside structural enzyme system in the bacterial respiratory chain (Supplementary Fig. S7).

Next, the bacterial killing kinetics of Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs are studied using S. aureus and E. coli. As shown in Fig. 2(B and C), both CQDs can rapidly eradicate living bacteria. The bacterial killing activity displays time dependence. Lys-CQDs can completely inactivate all S. aureus within 1 h at a concentration of 500 μg/mL. In comparison, the bactericidal rate of Arg-CQDs is slightly slower than that of Lys-CQDs. This result may be due to the higher ζ potential of Lys-CQDs, which causes faster and stronger interactions with the bacteria. In addition, both CQDs are more effective at killing S. aureus than E. coli, which is probably due to the different compositions of the Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacterial cells.

It is known that CQDs have large edge eﬀ ;ects and quantum confinement, which result in unique electron transport property [43]. The so-formed electron-hole pairs can react with surrounding molecular oxygen or water to form various types of ROS. For example, the electrons that are transiently trapped at the surface or subsurface defects can react with oxygen molecules to form superoxide anions (O2
−), while the holes can react with water molecules to form hydroxyl radicals (
OH) [25,44]. To explore the antibacterial mechanism of CQDs, we checked the level of reactive oxygen species (ROS), excess of which can damage proteins, lipids, metabolites and nucleotides [45,46], in bacteria. Clearly, the intracellular ROS levels in E. coli and S. aureus remarkably increased upon exposure to CQDs (Fig. 3 A and Supplementary Fig. S8), which is in agreement with previous studies using N-containing CQDs [24,25]. The fluorescence emission intensity of bacteria was generally higher after treatment with Lys-CQDs compared to those treated with Arg-CQDs, again implying the higher bactericidal ability of Lys-CQDs compared to Arg-CQDs. Considering the possible toxicity of CQDs to mammalian cells, intracellular ROS levels in NIH 3T3 cells and RBCs under CQD treatment were also investigated (Supplementary Fig. S9). Apparently, the levels of ROS in bacteria and NIH 3T3 cells were higher than that in RBCs after CQD treatment. Further, the influence of different CQD concentrations on intracellular ROS of NIH 3T3 cells was studied using flow cytometry (Supplementary Fig. S10). The level of ROS induced by CQDs was at the same order of magnitude as the positive control group (i.e., treated with H2O2) and no obvious differences was detected at various concentrations.

To elucidate the possible mechanism of ROS variations caused by CQDs in bacteria and mammalian cells, we further meansured the activities of two typical antioxidant enzymes which protect cells from oxidative damage by modulating the intracellular ROS levels [47]. Among them, superoxide dismutase (SOD) converts superoxides to hydrogen peroxide, which is less active than superoxides. Subsequently, catalase (CAT) turns hydrogen peroxide into H2O and O2, which are deemed nontoxic to cells and tissues [48]. As shown in Fig. 3B and Fig. S8, both Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs effectively inhibited the total SOD activities of E. coli and S. aureus. The inhibition rate increased from 20% to 80% when CQD concentration was increased from 0 to 500 μg/mL. Similarly, CQDs also inhibited CAT activity in bacteria (Fig. 3C and Fig. S8C). At high CQD concentrations, CAT activity was almost completely inhibited in bacteria. However, CQDs had no significant inhibitory effects on the antioxidant enzymes in cells such as NIH 3T3 cells and RBCs (Fig. 3B and C). Indeed, Arg-CQDs even enhanced SOD and CAT activities of cells within a certain range of CQD concentration. Therefore, inhibition of total SOD and CAT activities significantly varied between bacteria and cells, leading to markedly higher ROS level in bacteria than in cells. While further in-depth investigation is needed, it is likely that the SOD and CAT in mammalian cells have good tolerance to CQDs so they remain capable of protecting cells from ROS damage, while the two antioxidants in bacteria might be sensitive to CQDs or even become deactivated and fail to protect bacteria. In brief, the differential inhibition efficiency on the antioxidant enzymes in bacteria and mammalian cells likely contribute to the seemingly contradictive antibacterial and biocompatible properties of CQDs.

Currently, bacterial resistance is one of the major concerns of modern healthcare. To assess the potential of bacteria developing resistance to Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs after a long-lasting antibacterial treatment, both E. coli and S. aureus are incubated in the presence of half inhibitory concentrations of antibacterial agents each day, and the antibacterial activity is measured through 14 passages. As depicted in Fig. 4 A, the MIC of Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs to both S. aureus and E. coli do not change significantly after 14 passages, which indicates that CQDs do not trigger resistance in S. aureus and E. coli. For comparison, the commercial available antibiotic, Rifampicin rapidly leads to resistance of S. aureus and E. coli after incubated for the 6th and 10th generations, respectively. These results suggest that Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs do not tend to develop drug resistance.

In addition, the formation of bacterial biofilm is an urgent problem in clinical and nosocomial infection due to its inherent resistance to antibiotics [49]. To investigate the antibiofilm performance of the as-prepared CQDs, we culture S. aureus following the standard method to form biofilms with various concentrations of CQD treatment. As seen from Fig. 4B, the SEM images show that, in the untreated group, the S. aureus forms a wide range of dense and robust membranes on a coverslip after 48 h of culturing. By adding CQDs, the bacterial biofilms are significantly reduced and exhibit a detached island-like morphology. Confocal scanning laser microscopy (CLSM) further supports that biofilms thin with increasing CQD concentration. Moreover, many red colonies are observed, suggesting dead bacteria in biofilms. The crystal violet staining results (Fig. 4C) show that the inhibition efficiencies on biofilms increase with increasing CQD concentration up to 94.0% and 90.6% at 2000 μg/mL for Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs, respectively. These results confirm that the as-prepared CQDs are significantly effective in preventing S. aureus biofilms formation.

From the above results, the lysine and arginine residues of CQDs partially contribute to the antibacterial activities of CQDs, which are similar to natural and mimicking antimicrobial peptides [32,33]. The CQDs with lysine or arginine residues are positively charged under neutral conditions (Supplementary Fig. S4). The heads of the acidic phospholipids of bacterial cell membranes are negatively charged, which attracts positively charged CQDs, binding CQDs to the bacterial cell membranes through strong electrostatic interactions. Then, the amino acid residues insert into the phospholipid membranes [32]. This phenomenon is similar to putting many electric orbs into the bacterial membrane. Additionally, the binding of CQDs to the surface of the bacteria can change the potential difference between the inside and outside of the bacterial cell membrane, leading to dysfunction of the ion channels in the cell membrane, disorder of the ion balance in the cells, and eventually causing bacterial death [26]. It is worth mentioning that amino acid residues are prone to be protonated under acidic conditions, which leads to higher zeta potentials. Consequently, the CQDs exhibit greater antibacterial abilities in acidic conditions than neutral conditions. This acid-enhanced antibacterial activity of CQDs is very important in anti-infection applications since infected area mostly presents acid local environment.

On the other hand, the antibacterial activity can be mostly ascribed to the ROS produced by CQDs. Both Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs can induce the generation of damaging ROS in bacteria (Fig. 3A). Moreover, the total SOD and CAT activities of bacteria are efficiently inhibited by CQDs (Fig. 3B and C). Thus, bacteria cells do not have high enough antioxidant capacities to remove large amounts of intracellular ROS and become more susceptible to oxidant-induced damage. In addition, ROS is prone to decompose extracellular polymeric substances (EPS), which is considered as the major component of microbial biofilm communities to protect pathogenic bacteria. Thus, the CQDs prepared effectively prevent biofilms formation (Fig. 4B and C).

Taken together, these results confirm that CQDs can disrupt the integrity of the membrane and eventually lead to bacterial death by the combined action of the cations and ROS. Moreover, the zeta potential of Lys-CQDs is more positive than that of Arg-CQDs, and the ROS content of the bacteria exposed to Lys-CQDs is higher than that of Arg-CQDs. Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs have similar inhibition effects on antioxidant substances in bacteria. Therefore, Lys-CQDs show relatively stronger antimicrobial and antibiofilm properties than Arg-CQDs. However, the exact mechanisms of the bactericidal activities of CQDs prepared should be investigated further.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro hemolytic activity, cytotoxicity and cell selectivity
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned above, our CQDs show significant antibacterial properties. Furthermore, an in vitro hemolysis assay is performed in defibrinated human blood to elucidate the biocompatibility of CQDs on red blood cells (RBCs) in biological solution. The spectrophotometric analysis of the supernatants and photographs of treated RBCs are collected after the RBCs are exposed to various concentrations of CQDs (0–4000 μg/mL) for 1 and 24 h (Fig. 5 ). Significantly, the supernatant of the RBC suspension was still clear up to a measured concentration of 4000 μg/mL. It means no hemolysis at any tested concentration. This result manifests the highly selective antibacterial activities of the prepared CQDs on bacteria over RBCs (Table 1). Thus, this result implies that our CQDs would hardly induce hemolysis during antibacterial administration.

Although the previously reported CQDs showed low toxicity, a slightly inhibitory effect on mammalian cell proliferation was clearly observed [16]. Herein, we perform a standard CCK-8 assays to evaluate the cytocompatibility of CQDs prepared using NIH 3T3 cells as an example. Surprising, CCK-8 results show that the survival rate of NIH 3T3 cells increased with the addition of CQDs and kept increasing within a certain concentration range (Fig. 6 A). Importantly, the proliferation-accelerating lasted during the whole culture duration. After seven days of culture, the cell viability values of both Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs groups increased approximately seven-fold at maximum compared with that of the control. Further, Live/Dead staining assays were employed to investigate the effect of CQDs on cell viability, which confirmed the results of CCK-8 tests (Fig. 6B). the fluorescence images of the cell proliferation. Moreover, the NIH 3T3 cells still exhibit green fluorescence and normal shapes after incubation for 24 h with CQDs. It indicates that both Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs do not cause any side effects such as deformities or inactivation. These results suggested that the CQDs had no apparent toxicity on NIH 3T3 cells and on the contrary, they promoted cell proliferation. We further checked the effects of CQDs on the growth of two additional types of cells, bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells (BMSCs) and human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs). Apparently, these CQDs also effectively promoted the growth of BMSCs and HUVECs, just as good as they did on NIH 3T3 cells (Supplementary Figs. S11 and S12). Therefore, both Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs appeared to have no apparent cytotoxicity and indeed, might promote cell proliferation.

Considering both clinical safety and efficacy, an ideal antibacterial agent should discriminate between bacteria and mammalian cells. Here, we observe the surprising result of our CQDs exhibiting antibacterial properties and promoting mammalian cells growth at the same time. This is an unprecedented result in the context of reported antimicrobial agents.

As shown earlier in this paper, the surface charges of resultant CQDs are moderately positive with values less than 20 mV at neutral pH. As the bacterial membrane surface is more negatively charged than mammalian cells [33], CQDs exhibit different affinity to bacterial and mammalian cells via electrostatic interaction (Scheme 1 ). Therefore, CQDs preferentially interact with bacterial cells over mammalian cells, allowing CQDs to disrupt bacterial membrane and kill microorganisms. Our Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs, having moderate positive charges which are much less than spermidine [26,27], present weak electrostatic interactions with mammalian cells, which may account for their selectivity toward bacteria over cells. Moreover, the cells, containing more cholesterol in the plasma membrane to strengthen membrane integrity, are more resistant to the destructive potential of CQDs.

An appropriate increase in ROS can promote cell proliferation and differentiation, while excessive ROS causes cell senescence and even cell death [30,31]. ROS can mainly contribute to the selectivity of CQDs through different effects on bacterial and mammalian cells. As mentioned previously, CQDs prepared induce the production of ROS after exposure to bacteria, NIH 3T3 and RBCs (Fig. 3A and Fig. S9). For the relatively low level of ROS in the RBCs, all CQDs demonstrated no hemolytic toxicity. The amounts of ROS in bacteria and NIH 3T3 were of the same order of magnitude. However, the SOD and CAT bioactivities in bacteria were effectively inhibited by CQDs. Thus, bacteria were not fully competent at removing the ROS generated by Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs. While high level of ROS destroyed bacterial cell membrane, mammalian cells were able to initiate antioxidant systems (Fig. 3) to remove excessive ROS and control the ROS level under contact with CQDs (Scheme 1). Furthermore, the formation of ROS includes the production of superoxides, which is highly unstable and reacts with lipids causing peroxidation and oxidative damage to DNA and proteins [50]. With these superoxides being converted to relatively low-activity hydrogen peroxide by antioxidant enzymes, the oxidant damage capacity of ROS also decreases. Thus, our CQDs are not harmful and have no side effects on cells. Intrinsically generated ROS is believed to be a major inducing trigger and apoptotic effector molecule in the action of cell stress. Exposing dividing mammalian cells to the environment with an appropriate increasing of hydrogen peroxide actually stimulates cell mitosis. It’s therefore very likely that Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs do not cause destructive oxidative stress upon contacting with cells; instead, they may behave as mitogenic signaling agents within the concentrations tested to promote cell proliferation and differentiation.

Though deeper understanding in the unusual properties of CQDs prepared is needed, we believe that unique CQDs are obtained by deriving from lysine and arginine, combining the best of both worlds: effective bacterial killing activity and significant cell growth accelerating action, which would open up the potential use of these CQDs in many biomedical applications for simplifying treatment process and saving enormous medical costs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Wound healing application and in vivo antibacterial ability
ce:section.ce:para: It is generally accepted that neither antimicrobial materials nor tissue regeneration materials could control infection and promote wound healing at the same time [7]. In this paper, the as-prepared CQDs have been shown to kill bacteria and promote the proliferation of mouse fibroblasts without causing hemolysis. To verify the practicability of Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs for clinical applications, we also conduct an evaluation of the healing of wounds in bacterially infected mice. Fig. 7 A shows photographs of wounds on the backs of mice from the nine groups during the therapeutic process. The wounds without antibacterial agents (a, b, and c) have different degrees of erythema and edema. Only slight improvements are seen in the positive control, 3 M bandage groups (d and e). In marked contrast, Lys-CQDs (f and g) and Arg-CQDs (h and i) show significant improvement in the healing of the wounds. After three days of therapy, the CQD treatment groups show greater epithelial coverage than the other groups, and on the 5th day, CQDs groups show nearly complete healing. In order to demonstrate the changes of wound size more clearly, we traced the wound-bed closure during 5 days for each treatment (Supplementary Fig. S13). Clearly, the wound size treated with CQDs became smaller than that of other groups at each day. The wound infected by S. aureus and treated with Arg-CQDs was almost complete repair. To further investigate the wound healing effect of the CQDs, the wound contraction of each group was determined at 1st day, 3rd day and 5th day, respectively (Supplementary Fig. S14). Compared with the control groups, the CQD-treated groups exhibited larger wound contraction area after 5 days of treatment, indicating their better wound healing performance.

Histological analysis of the postoperative wound healing on the backs of the mice is carried out by hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining (Fig. 7B). The boundary between the wound without drugs administered (a, b, and c) and adjacent normal tissue can be clearly distinguished, which indicates that the degree of wound healing is low. The bacteria have affected the dynamic healing process of the wound. In the band-aid treatment groups (d and e), migration of keratinocytes is observed. The wound surfaces in the CQD treatment group (f, g, h, and i) are smooth and uniform, and the degree of wound healing is much higher than that of the band-aid groups (d and e). More blood vessels (yellow arrows) and hair follicles (red arrows) are developed at the wound sites of the CQD treatment groups, and the wound crusts appear to be the same as the normal tissue (j). These results indicate enhanced healing of the wounds by CQDs.

To investigate the in vivo antibacterial efficacy of CQDs in the wound tissue of mice, bacteria from the infected tissues are homogenized and cultured in Luria-Bertani (LB) medium. The data are presented in the form of a histogram (Fig. 7C). In the infected sites, the numbers of viable bacteria in the wounds treated with CQDs are much lower than those in the untreated groups and even lower than the positive control (3 M antibacterial bandage groups) after five days of therapy. The significant reduction of the survival of bacteria in infected tissues is mainly attributed to the persistent antibacterial activity of CQDs, and the antibacterial advantage over 3 M bandage is exhibited along with the lasting administration. Wounds are prone to infection with bacteria, providing bacteria with conditions to form biofilms, which leads to sepsis and even death [51]. Our CQDs have the ability to kill bacteria on the wounds and inhibit biofilms, and may therefore be a potential candidate for controlling infection with excellent antibacterial efficiency.

With the formation of wound, the local content of H2O2 is higher than that of normal tissue (Fig. 7D). Although a low concentration of H2O2 helps the healing process at the wound site, the wound in the untreated group (a) was still not healing after 5 d. After infection with bacteria (b and c), the situation was more serious. Correspondingly, a high content of H2O2 was detected in the untreated group and band-aid treated groups (d, e). However, at 5th day, the CQD-treated groups (f, g, h, and i) apparently showed faster wound healing and less H2O2 presence than other injury groups. Such considerable antibacterial performance and wound healing might be attributed to synergy between CQDs and H2O2. The local excessive H2O2 due to the wound can lead to oxidative damage of both the normal tissue cell and bacteria. However, CQDs can inhibit the activity of the SOD and CAT in bacteria, resulting in excess ROS that cannot be removed. On the contrary, the inhibitory effect of CQDs in cells was rarely seen. In certain circumstances, Arg-CQDs even promoted cell growth, implying that the cells might be capable of removing excessive ROS. Hence, the selective inhibition of the SOD and CAT bioactivities plays a more important role in selectively killing bacteria and promoting the proliferation of mammalian cells. In addition, it can be observed that no chronic inflammation was initiated in the wound sites. Therefore, both Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs showed excellent antibacterial properties and meanwhile promoted wound healing in mice.

Wound healing is a dynamic process consisting of three phases that overlap in time and space, including inflammation, cell proliferation and tissue remodeling [52]. Many factors can interfere with one or more phases of the process, resulting in abnormal wound healing or injury [53]. In a wound infected with bacteria, the removal of contaminating microorganisms is important. If no effective decontamination is performed, inflammation is prolonged due to the formation of bacterial biofilms at the wound site. Antibacterial materials could effectively prevent the wound from severe bacteria infection. However, the toxicity toward host cells limits their ability to promote the healing of infected wounds. Our results indicate that Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs display effective decontamination and antibiofilm actions that eliminate inflammation. During the cell proliferation phase, CQDs can promote cell proliferation and accelerate wound healing. Consequently, the tissue remodeling is also enhanced. Therefore, in light of their considerable antibacterial activity and cell growth-promoting ability, the Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs in this study can be used as effective wound dressings which simultaneously treat bacterial infections and expedite wound healing.

In summary, we have successfully developed two types of CQDs, Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs, as novel bactericides through pyrolysis of the small-molecules l-lysine and l-arginine, respectively. These CQDs possessed effective and selective antibacterial capability without apparently inducing drug resistance in E. coli and S. aureus. The cationic residues and excess intracellular ROS induced by CQDs might account for their antibacterial effect. On the contrary, in vitro cytotoxicity tests showed that both Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs promoted the growth of typical mammalian cells, which might be partially due to the moderate positive charges of CQDs. Further, it was found that the CQD-resulted ROS might, through differential regulation by the antioxidant enzymes SOD and CAT, mainly contribute to the selectivity of CQDs toward bacteria and mammalian cells. Consequenstly, Lys-CQDs and Arg-CQDs were also applied to treating wounds in mouse models and showed antibacterial effect and faciliated wound healing. Together, in this study we have realized a combination of desirable antibacterial activity and cell growth-promoting capability in a single nanoparticle using CQDs, which may markedly avoid the side effects of traditional antibacterial agents and may therefore lead to important clinical applications of CQDs.
The authors declare no conflict of interest.

Peili Li: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Fengxuan Han: Investigation, Data curation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Weiwei Cao: Investigation, Formal analysis. Gaoke Zhang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Jiaying Li: Investigation, Formal analysis. Jinwei Zhou: Investigation, Formal analysis. Xuedong Gong: Investigation, Formal analysis. Gareth Turnbull: Writing - review & editing. Wenmiao Shu: Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Lunguo Xia: Methodology. Bing Fang: Conceptualization, Supervision. Xiaodong Xing: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Bin Li: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Methodology, Writing - review & editing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Peili Li and Dr. Fengxuan Han equally contributed to this work. This study was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (81460107, 31530024, 31872748, 81925027), National Key R&D Program of China (2016YFC1100203), Jiangsu Provincial Special Program of Medical Science (BL2012004) and the Priority Academic Program Development (PAPD) of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apmt.2020.100601.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Versatile antibacterial materials: an emerging arsenal for combatting bacterial pathogens
Antibacterial resistance worldwide: causes, challenges and responses
Nanotechnology as a therapeutic tool to combat microbial resistance
Cationic amphiphilic non-hemolytic polyacrylates with superior antibacterial activity
Green fabrication of amphiphilic quaternized β-chitin derivatives with excellent biocompatibility and antibacterial activities for wound healing
Balancing antimicrobial activity with biological safety: bifunctional chitosan derivative for the repair of wounds with Gram-positive bacterial infections
Lipase-sensitive polymeric triple-layered nanogel for “on-demand” drug delivery
Bactericidal activity of partially oxidized nanodiamonds
Hollow fiber membrane decorated with Ag/MWNTs: toward effective water disinfection and biofouling control
Mechanisms of the antimicrobial activities of graphene materials
The antibacterial applications of graphene and its derivatives
Toxic potential of materials at the nanolevel
Cytotoxicity effects of graphene and single-wall carbon nanotubes in neural phaeochromocytoma-derived PC12 cells
Carbon quantum dots and their applications
Charge-convertible carbon dots for imaging-guided drug delivery with enhanced in vivo cancer therapeutic efficiency
Carbon dots based FRET for the detection of DNA damage
Carbon quantum dots with photoenhanced hydrogen-bond catalytic activity in aldol condensations
53% Efficient red emissive carbon quantum dots for high color rendering and stable warm white-light-emitting diodes
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning, sensors, and bioimaging
Imaging biofilm-encased microorganisms using carbon dots derived from L. plantarum
Visible-light-activated bactericidal functions of carbon “Quantum” dots
Programmed bacteria death induced by carbon dots with different surface charge
S-and N-doped carbon quantum dots: surface chemistry dependent antibacterial activity
Super-cationic carbon quantum dots synthesized from spermidine as an eye drop formulation for topical treatment of bacterial keratitis
Synthesis of self-assembled spermidine-carbon quantum dots effective against multidrug-resistant bacteria
Carbon dot-based platform for simultaneous bacterial distinguishment and antibacterial applications
Multifunctional silicon-carbon nanohybrids simultaneously featuring bright fluorescence, high antibacterial and wound healing activity
Reactive oxygen species generated in different compartments induce cell death, survival, or senescence
SENP3-mediated de-conjugation of SUMO2/3 from promyelocytic leukemia is correlated with accelerated cell proliferation under mild oxidative stress
Antimicrobial peptides: general overview and clinical implications in human health and disease
Antimicrobial peptide mimicking primary amine and guanidine containing methacrylamide copolymers prepared by raft polymerization
Fabrication of charge reversible graphene oxide-based nanocomposite with multiple antibacterial modes and magnetic recyclability
Graphene quantum dots as autophagy-inducing photodynamic agents
